articles.headline: This Is Why Boeing Stopped Producing The 757
articles.blurb: The iconic aircraft was once a workhorse of many fleets around the world.
articles.takeaway: Boeing's 757 discontinuation reflects market shift toward larger twin-aisle aircraft and the challenge of sustaining single-aisle production lines amid competitive pressures.

articles.takeaway: Boeing 777X and Airbus A350-1000 are emerging as viable A380 replacements, prioritizing fuel efficiency and operational flexibility over absolute passenger capacity.

articles.headline: United Airlines To Debut New Polaris Suites Without Full Passenger Privacy

articles.headline: The Evolution of Aviation SMS
articles.blurb: From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

articles.headline: Delta Air Lines Passenger Yells Racial Slur At Flight Attendant, Faces Up To 20 Years In Prison

articles.headline: Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization
articles.blurb: Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.
articles.takeaway: Operational optimization through advanced technologies offers airlines, airports, and flight operators the critical pathway to balance growing air traffic demand with environmental sustainability.
keywords: aviation; optimization; operations research; sustainability; environmental impacts

articles.headline: Why Do Pilots Use The Term "Roger" Instead Of "Yes"?
articles.blurb: Roger was one of the first words for 'R' in early aviation phonetic alphabets and it went on to mean 'message received.'
articles.takeaway: Roger originated as the phonetic alphabet word for 'R' and evolved to mean message received, establishing critical radio communication clarity.
